caveats: a: The BLS occupation table is for broader NAICS 221100 and May 2023 employment, not the narrowed operator lens.
a: Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and do not reveal what is already automated.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input has a vintage mismatch and may understate or overstate the relevant labor base for small operators.
rho: DOE describes opportunities and priorities rather than realized labor implementation.
rho: Adoption can differ sharply by utility scale, grid condition, data architecture, and state regulatory regime.
rho: Critical-infrastructure cybersecurity and human-in-the-loop requirements may limit labor removal even when tools are deployed.
e: No source directly measures the share of frozen LMM firms meeting all lens conditions.
e: The frozen count is margin-bridged from establishment and receipts data and can misclassify regulated utilities with atypical capital structures or accounting.
e: EIA's ownership counts describe utilities rather than the Census firm universe used for the frozen count.
s5: No industry-specific denominator of eligible independent firms and qualifying transfers was found.
s5: State approval rules and buyer concentration create substantial geographic variation.
s5: Observed utility M&A can overrepresent large systems and parent-company deals that do not fit the lens.
q: Retention is jurisdiction-specific and depends on rate-case timing and tariff design.
q: The cited regulatory structure is directional and not a measurement of retained AI savings.
q: Competitive retail or contract arrangements can reprice differently from regulated delivery service.
d5: EIA projections are national scenarios rather than forecasts for small private distribution systems.
d5: Electricity consumption is not identical to constant-quality distribution-service demand.
d5: Weather, large-load interconnections, and distributed resources can dominate five-year outcomes in a small territory.
o: The range is a bounded judgment rather than an observed operator-required share.
o: The narrowed physical-operator lens mechanically produces higher operator requirement than the broker segment that was excluded.
o: Rapid adoption of durable behind-the-meter self-supply could lower the share in selected territories.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring regulated or contract-based charges to residential, commercial, and industrial customers for electricity delivery and associated distribution service, commonly recovered through approved tariffs and usage-linked charges.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 221122 combines physical distribution-system operators with brokers and agents that arrange electricity sales over others' systems. The lens is narrowed to private physical-system operators because the two models have materially different assets, regulation, labor workflows, and transfer mechanics; the narrowing is for coherence rather than attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Publicly managed utilities, member-owned cooperatives without a transferable controlling owner, captive distribution units, generation-only and transmission-only businesses, electricity brokers and agents, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Privately controlled lower-middle-market operators of physical electric distribution systems that repeatedly deliver electricity and related distribution service to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment by occupation for NAICS 221100, the broader electric power generation, transmission, and distribution group, rather than wage-weighted task exposure for NAICS 221122. Management is 8.79% of employment, production is 11.25%, and construction and extraction is 5.12%; these shares anchor a task-level judgment that administrative, customer, planning, and analytical work is more exposed than field repair and switching. Inclusion of generation and transmission occupations can bias the estimate downward if distribution has more customer-service work, or upward if the narrowed small-operator lens relies more heavily on field crews.
caveats: The BLS occupation table is for broader NAICS 221100 and May 2023 employment, not the narrowed operator lens.
Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and do not reveal what is already automated.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input has a vintage mismatch and may understate or overstate the relevant labor base for small operators.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted staffing census from small private distribution utilities mapped to O*NET tasks, with current automation status for each task, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: Billing support, customer communications, meter-data review, outage triage, load forecasting, inspection review, work-order preparation, procurement, and regulatory reporting contain language and analytical tasks that AI can partly substitute or use to avoid hiring. Live electrical work, emergency restoration, physical inspection, switching authority, and safety-critical decisions remain substantially human.

d5.bridge: EIA projects aggregate U.S. electricity demand and capacity rather than constant-price, constant-quality demand for the narrowed distribution-service basket. The five-year ratio translates national power-demand growth into distribution throughput, with a lower bound allowing efficiency and behind-the-meter supply and an upper bound allowing concentrated electrification and data-center load. National demand can bias a local utility estimate in either direction because service territories have different population, industrial, and distributed-energy trends.
caveats: EIA projections are national scenarios rather than forecasts for small private distribution systems.
Electricity consumption is not identical to constant-quality distribution-service demand.
Weather, large-load interconnections, and distributed resources can dominate five-year outcomes in a small territory.
d5.change_evidence: Service-territory load forecasts from integrated resource plans and distribution-system plans, separated into billed energy, peak demand, customer count, distributed generation, and constant-quality delivery requirements, would materially move the ratio.
d5.rationale: Electrification, data centers, and economic activity support delivered-electricity demand, while efficiency, customer-sited generation, storage, and geographic load shifts temper the result. Physical distribution capacity and reliability service remain needed even where net energy delivered grows slowly.

caveats: No source directly measures the share of frozen LMM firms meeting all lens conditions.
The frozen count is margin-bridged from establishment and receipts data and can misclassify regulated utilities with atypical capital structures or accounting.
EIA's ownership counts describe utilities rather than the Census firm universe used for the frozen count.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level reconciliation of Census entities to EIA Form 861 respondents, ownership type, ultimate parent, physical distribution assets, normalized EBITDA, and control-transferability would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The frozen firm count covers the whole NAICS code, while the coherent lens excludes brokers, public utilities, non-transferable cooperatives, captive units, subsidiaries lacking independent control, and firms outside the specified EBITDA band after normalization. EIA's ownership evidence and the service-territory structure imply that only a minority of counted firms are plausible privately controlled physical-system operators available for a control transaction.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 221122 ($30,789,618,323) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 221122 ($392,305,682,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3492 (margins.json[22]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Utility (General) EBITDA/Sales 34.92%); revenue/employee $1,106,308 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 221122); class <5: 256 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,807,777 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,726,476 -> IN $1-10M band; class 5-9: 63 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,399,683 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,329,969 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 123 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,551,024 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,430,418 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 564 firms, avg revenue/firm $50,319,420 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,571,542 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 204 firms, avg revenue/firm $231,873,181 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $80,970,115 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 80 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,899,133,162 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,361,577,300 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 442

caveats: The range is a bounded judgment rather than an observed operator-required share.
The narrowed physical-operator lens mechanically produces higher operator requirement than the broker segment that was excluded.
Rapid adoption of durable behind-the-meter self-supply could lower the share in selected territories.
o.change_evidence: Distribution-plan data on grid defections, durable islanding, microgrid operation, utility customer exits, and the share of network functions fully transferred to customers or third parties would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Nearly all surviving demand for the current physical distribution basket still requires an accountable operator to maintain conductors and substations, balance local constraints, meter delivery, restore outages, satisfy reliability obligations, and coordinate safety. Software can automate decisions and customer interactions but does not eliminate accountability for the energized network; only customer self-supply, microgrids, or service-basket migration removes a limited share.

caveats: Retention is jurisdiction-specific and depends on rate-case timing and tariff design.
The cited regulatory structure is directional and not a measurement of retained AI savings.
Competitive retail or contract arrangements can reprice differently from regulated delivery service.
q.change_evidence: State-specific tariff language, recent rate-case orders, earnings-sharing mechanisms, productivity offsets, test-year conventions, and management interviews on the treatment of operating-cost savings would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Regulated tariffs and rate cases can eventually share operating efficiencies with customers, while regulatory lag, fixed-price arrangements, incentive mechanisms, and below-threshold cost changes can let an operator retain part of an implemented benefit. The estimate is discounted over five years and excludes demand and implementation effects.

rho.bridge: DOE identifies distribution-grid applications for AI, machine learning, predictive diagnostics, and automation, but does not measure the five-year implementation share of the exposed labor opportunity at small private utilities. The range translates technical applicability into operational adoption after integration, validation, cybersecurity, safety, and regulatory constraints. A federal technology roadmap may bias implementation upward relative to resource-constrained small operators.
caveats: DOE describes opportunities and priorities rather than realized labor implementation.
Adoption can differ sharply by utility scale, grid condition, data architecture, and state regulatory regime.
Critical-infrastructure cybersecurity and human-in-the-loop requirements may limit labor removal even when tools are deployed.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort study of small distribution utilities reporting deployed use cases, workflow penetration, pre-deployment staffing, post-deployment staffing, exception rates, and regulator or insurer acceptance would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Customer, back-office, planning-support, and document workflows can be implemented with review controls, while operational-technology integration and safety-critical decisions face longer validation cycles. Legacy systems, data quality, cybersecurity, reliability obligations, and procurement slow conversion of technical possibility into labor substitution.

caveats: No industry-specific denominator of eligible independent firms and qualifying transfers was found.
State approval rules and buyer concentration create substantial geographic variation.
Observed utility M&A can overrepresent large systems and parent-company deals that do not fit the lens.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census matching EIA utility identifiers to S&P Capital IQ, PitchBook, state commission change-of-control dockets, and ultimate-parent records would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Qualifying transfers are possible for the small subset of independently controlled private utilities, but ownership continuity, regulated service territories, approval requirements, capital intensity, and strategic-buyer participation make control sales less routine than ordinary owner-operated service-company succession. The range excludes public reorganizations, cooperative governance changes, and parent-level transactions that do not transfer the operating firm.

principal_driver: Essential physical-network accountability and durable electricity demand support recurring operator-required service.
principal_weakness: Only a small, poorly measured share of the apparent firm population is likely to be a private, independent, transferable physical distribution operator.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments operating electric distribution systems or acting as brokers and agents arranging electricity sales over systems operated by others.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS 221122: Electric Power Distribution

sources.supports: Reports 393,880 employees in the broader electric power generation, transmission, and distribution industry, including 34,620 in management, 20,170 in construction and extraction, 44,310 in production, and 32,100 power plant operators, distributors, and dispatchers.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 221100

sources.supports: DOE describes distribution-grid digitization needs and recommends expanded use of artificial intelligence and machine learning for predictive diagnostics and automation; it also identifies the distribution grid as the location of most outages.
sources.title: Last Mile Digitization for Resilience

sources.supports: Reports almost 3,000 U.S. electric distribution companies in 2017 and distinguishes investor-owned, publicly managed, and cooperative ownership; investor-owned utilities served 72% of customers.
sources.title: Investor-owned utilities served 72% of U.S. electricity customers in 2017

sources.supports: EIA's Annual Energy Outlook 2026 projects average annual U.S. electricity demand growth of 0.9% to 1.6% through 2050 across modeled cases and substantial installed-capacity growth.
sources.title: EIA projects growing U.S. electricity demand and capacity through 2050

sources.supports: Describes investor-owned utilities' geographic service monopolies, obligation to serve, state and federal regulation, and approval of rates intended to permit a fair return, supporting the structural discussion of control transfers and benefit pass-through.
sources.title: The Changing Structure of the Electric Power Industry: An Update

title: Electric Power Distribution
